sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: SPACE.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 33, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 32 of about 71 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! After pressing SPACE:
- Player green body at rows 28-31, cols 20-22 (unchanged)
- White extension shrunk from 16 pixels to 4 pixels (node 3: W, pixels=4, boundary=[[28, 23], [31, 23]])
- The left blue center (node 6) is still at rows 29-30, cols 17-18 (unchanged)
- The carried blue center (node 7) is still at rows 29-30, cols 25-26 (unchanged)
- The left blue square frame (node 1) is still there at rows 28-31, cols 16-19
So SPACE didn't collect the left blue center! Instead, it SHRUNK the white extension from 16 pixels back to 4 pixels. This is the opposite of what happened at the right blue square.
Wait, let me think about this more carefully. At the right blue square:
- Player was at rows 24-27, cols 40-43 (green at 40-42, white at 43)
- Right blue square frame was at rows 24-27, cols 44-47
- After SPACE, white extension grew from 4 pixels to 16 pixels (cols 43-47)
- The right blue center was at rows 25-26, cols 45-46, which is INSIDE the new white extension
At the left blue square:
- Player is at rows 28-31, cols 20-27 (green at 20-22, white at 23-27)
- Left blue square frame is at rows 28-31, cols 16-19
- After SPACE, white extension shrank from 16 pixels to 4 pixels (col 23)
- The left blue center is at rows 29-30, cols 17-18, which is NOT inside the white extension
So SPACE seems to work differently depending on the direction. When the player is to the LEFT of a blue square frame (facing right), SPACE extends the white part to the RIGHT, collecting the blue center. When the player is to the RIGHT of a blue square frame (facing left), SPACE... shrinks the white part?
Wait, maybe SPACE always extends the white part in the direction the player is "facing". The player's "face" is the direction of the white extension. When the player moved to the right blue square, the white extension was on the right side (col 43), so SPACE extended it further right. When the player moved to the left blue square, the white extension was on the right side (cols 23-27), but the blue square was on the LEFT. So SPACE tried to extend to the right (away from the blue square) and... shrunk instead?
Actually, let me reconsider. Maybe SPACE extends the white part in the direction of the nearest blue square frame. At the right blue square, the frame was to the right, so SPACE extended right. At the left blue square, the frame is to the left, but the white part is on the right. So SPACE can't extend left because there's no white part on the left side.
Hmm, this is getting complicated. Let me think about it differently. Maybe the white part always extends in the direction the player last moved. When the player moved RIGHT to reach the right blue square, the white part was on the right. When the player moved LEFT to reach the left blue square, the white part should be on the left. But the white part is still on the right (cols 23-27).
Wait, actually, looking at the player's structure:
- Green body: cols 20-22
- White extension: col 23 (after SPACE)
The white extension is on the RIGHT side of the green body. So the player is "facing" right. When I pressed SPACE at the right blue square, the white part extended to the right (correct direction). When I pressed SPACE at the left blue square, the white part is on the right, but the blue square is on the left. So SPACE can't collect the left blue center because the player is facing the wrong way.
So maybe I need to reposition the player so that the white extension is on the LEFT side of the green body, facing the left blue square. But how do I change which side the white extension is on?
Actually, looking at the initial state, the player had the white extension on the right side (col 27). When the player moved, the white extension stayed on the right side. So the white extension is always on the right side of the green body, regardless of movement direction.
Wait, but when the player moved LEFT, the white extension should still be on the right side of the green body. Let me check:
- After LEFT x4: green at cols 24-26, white at cols 27-31. Yes, white is on the right.
- After DOWN, LEFT, LEFT: green at cols 20-22, white at cols 23-27. Yes, white is on the right.
So the white extension is always on the right side of the green body. This means the player is always "facing" right. To collect a blue center, the blue square frame must be to the RIGHT of the player.
For the left blue square at cols 16-19, the player needs to be to the LEFT of it, with the white extension pointing right toward the frame. So the player's green body should be at cols 12-14 (or similar), and the white extension at col 15, adjacent to the frame at cols 16-19.
But wait, the player is currently at cols 20-22 (green) + col 23 (white). The left blue square frame is at cols 16-19. The player is to the RIGHT of the frame, not to the left. So I need to move the player to the LEFT of the frame.
To get to the left of the frame, I need to move the player to cols 12-14 (green) + col 15 (white). Then the white extension would be adjacent to the frame at cols 16-19, and SPACE would extend the white part to the right, collecting the blue center.
But the player is currently at cols 20-23. To move to cols 12-15, I need to move LEFT twice. But the frame is at cols 16-19, so moving LEFT would put the player at cols 16-19, which overlaps with the frame. The frame might disappear when the player overlaps with it.
Actually, let me reconsider. The player is at cols 20-23. The frame is at cols 16-19. If I move LEFT once, the player goes to cols 16-19, which overlaps with the frame. The frame might disappear. If I move LEFT again, the player goes to cols 12-15, which is to the left of the frame.
But wait, the frame disappeared earlier when the player was adjacent to it (at the right blue square). So maybe the frame disappears when the player is adjacent or overlapping. Let me try moving LEFT once to see what happens.
Actually, I realize I've been wasting a lot of actions. Let me think about this more carefully.
The key insight is:
1. The player has a green body (4x3) and a white extension (4x1) on the right side.
2. SPACE extends the white extension to the right, collecting any blue center in its path.
3. After SPACE, the white extension grows to 4x4 (16 pixels).
4. When the player moves, the white extension moves with the player.
5. If SPACE is pressed when there's no blue square to the right, the white extension shrinks back to 4x1.
Wait, but at the left blue square, I pressed SPACE and the white extension shrunk. But there WAS a blue square to the left (not to the right). So SPACE only works when there's a blue square to the RIGHT of the player.
For the left blue square at cols 16-19, I need to position the player to the LEFT of it, so the blue square is to the RIGHT. The player's green body should be at cols 12-14, and the white extension at col 15. Then SPACE would extend the white part to cols 16-19, collecting the blue center.
But to get to cols 12-14, I need to move LEFT from the current position (cols 20-22). Moving LEFT once puts the player at cols 16-18, which overlaps with the frame. Moving LEFT again puts the player at cols 12-14.
Let me try moving LEFT twice and then pressing SPACE.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Player at rows 28-31, cols 20-23 (green at 20-22, white at 23)
# Left blue square frame at rows 28-31, cols 16-19
# The player needs to be to the LEFT of the frame to collect it with SPACE
# Let me move LEFT twice to get to cols 12-15, then press SPACE

result = action(['LEFT', 'LEFT', 'SPACE'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("After LEFT, LEFT, SPACE:")
for node in seg['nodes']:
    if node['color'] in ['N', 'W', 'b', 'c', 'g']:
        print(f"  id={node['id']}, color={node['color']}, pixels={node['pixels']}, boundary={node['boundary']}")
print("Score:", result.get('score'))
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
